title: Aion Durability — Event Sourcing, Replay, and Determinism
intention: Make Aion durable. Everything else in the engine is mechanics a non-durable orchestrator could also have; this layer is what lets a workflow crash on step nine of ten and resume exactly where it was, sleep for three months and resume in the same logical state, and run as if it ran once even after five replays. It does three things: it records (every externally-observable engine action becomes an Event appended to the EventStore under a single-writer-per-workflow discipline with an expected-sequence guard); it replays (re-runs the workflow function from the start, and for every world-touching call consults recorded history and returns the recorded outcome instead of acting again); and it enforces determinism (supplies the recorded timestamp for workflow.now, a workflow-seeded value for workflow.random, and detects when a replay diverges from recorded history — failing loudly rather than corrupting state silently). When done, the engine (AE) spawns a workflow process and hands it a resolver that transparently answers every world-touching call from history during replay and falls through to live execution at the resume point; recovery becomes list_active, replay each, resume. The workflow author writes a plain deterministic function and never knows whether a call executed for real or returned a cached result.
problem: Durable execution rests on one mechanism: a workflow function re-executed from the beginning must take the same path every time because every non-deterministic input is fed back from a recorded log. Any error here is silent and catastrophic — a card charged twice, an email sent twice, a reconstructed state that never existed.

Recording must be correct and exactly-once-per-action: scheduling an activity appends exactly one ActivityScheduled, completion exactly one ActivityCompleted, each with the right expected_seq, and there must be a single writer per workflow so sequence numbers stay monotonic and gap-free. A double-append, a wrong sequence, or a second writer all break replay.

Replay must match recorded results, not re-execute side effects: a workflow.run(activity) call during replay must look up the recorded outcome (Completed result, exhausted-retry Failed error) and only resume live where no event exists. Matching must be deterministic — the engine must know which recorded event corresponds to which call with no ambiguity. Signals, timers, and child workflows have the same shape.

Determinism inputs must come from history, never the environment: workflow.now must return the recorded timestamp of the event being processed, not the wall clock; workflow.random must be seeded deterministically from workflow identity so it yields the same sequence on every replay.

Divergence must be caught, not absorbed: accidentally non-deterministic workflow code reaches a call that does not match recorded history (asks for activity send-email where history records charge-card). This non-determinism violation must be detected at the point of mismatch and surfaced as a hard typed error, never silently papered over.
Recovery must be complete: on startup every non-terminal workflow must be replayed from full history and resumed at its resume point; missing one stalls a workflow forever.

If this layer is built loosely — ad-hoc call-to-history matching, a best-effort clock, a swallowed mismatch — durability is a lie. It must be a precise, fully-tested transcription of the event-sourcing model, with the violation path covered as rigorously as the happy path.

solution.summary: A durability module set inside the aion crate, aion::durability, depending on aion-core and aion-store, organised around four collaborating pieces. The Recorder is the single-writer append path that tracks a workflow's sequence head and appends events with the correct expected_seq; every world-touching engine action flows through exactly one Recorder per workflow. The HistoryCursor is an ordered, position-aware view over recorded events built once at replay start; it answers 'what is the next recorded outcome for a call of this kind and key?' and tracks exhaustion. The Resolver is the chokepoint the workflow consults for every world-touching command: it returns a recorded Resolution drawn from the cursor, reports ResumeLive when the cursor is exhausted, or raises NonDeterminismError when the next recorded event does not match the command. The DeterminismContext holds the recorded timestamp of the event currently being applied (the source for workflow.now) and a seeded deterministic RNG (the source for workflow.random), seeded from WorkflowId + RunId. Above these, replay drives a fresh execution through recorded history to the resume point, and recovery does list_active then replay-and-resume each active workflow on startup. The AD/AE seam is a LiveExecutor trait AE implements and AD invokes only after ResumeLive; AD never spawns a process or invents a sequence. The AD/AT seam: AT executes timers/signals/children live and tells the Recorder to append events, while AD replays their recorded forms without invoking AT. Matching is by deterministic correlation key (activities/children by ordinal-derived id, timers by TimerId, signals by name-in-order); violation detection is structural at the cursor position, never heuristic; activity-result caching is the history itself — the cursor is the cache.

decisions.title: Durability lives inside the aion crate, co-owned with AE
decisions.choice: aion::durability is a module set inside the aion crate, not a separate crate, because it is tightly coupled to the workflow process AE manages and to the engine's per-workflow context. The split with AE is by responsibility: AE owns lifecycle, process management, supervision, module loading, the public Engine API, and actually executing live actions; AD owns recording, replay, the Resolver, the determinism context, and recovery orchestration. The contract between them is a small trait set — AE implements LiveExecutor (which AD calls only on resume-live); AD provides Recorder/Resolver/replay/recover. Rejected a standalone aion-durability crate: it would need to expose the engine's per-workflow internals across a crate boundary for no isolation benefit, since clients/workers never link it anyway.

decisions.title: Single writer per workflow, structurally enforced by the Recorder
decisions.choice: Exactly one Recorder INSTANCE exists per active workflow and it is the only code path that appends to that workflow's history. Single-writer is by single instance, not single call site: both AD's resume-live handoff (command-issued events) and AT's live services (asynchronous-arrival events: TimerFired, SignalReceived, ChildWorkflowCompleted/Failed) append through that one Recorder, which is internally synchronized and is the sole sequence-head authority. The Recorder owns the tracked sequence head and computes every expected_seq from it, so concurrent callers are serialized and sequence numbers stay monotonic and gap-free. This makes the single-writer discipline structural rather than a convention. A SequenceConflict from the store therefore signals a bug (a rogue second Recorder), surfaced as a hard error, not retried by re-reading the head. Rejected free-floating append calls scattered across the engine, and rejected per-family Recorders: they would race on the sequence and corrupt the log.

decisions.title: Deterministic correlation keys match recorded events to calls
decisions.choice: Each world-touching command carries a correlation key deterministic across runs: activities and child workflows by an id derived from scheduling-sequence ordinal (the Nth scheduled is always N, per AC D4); timers by TimerId; signals by name in recorded order (the kth recorded SignalReceived of a name satisfies the kth receive of that name). The HistoryCursor walks history in sequence order and, for each command, returns the next recorded event of the matching family and key. Rejected content-hashing the call or relying on wall-clock ordering: ordinals are stable under replay precisely because workflow code is deterministic, which is the whole premise.

decisions.title: The Resolver is the single chokepoint: recorded, resume-live, or violation
decisions.choice: Every world-touching call goes through the Resolver, which yields exactly one of three outcomes against the cursor: (1) match found -> return the recorded Resolution and advance the determinism timestamp; (2) cursor exhausted -> ResumeLive, after which the engine executes for real and the Recorder appends; (3) mismatch (next recorded event differs in family or key) -> raise NonDeterminismError. Because all calls funnel through one place, recorded-vs-live and violation detection are decided in exactly one location, not duplicated per call kind.

decisions.title: Violation detection is structural, not heuristic
decisions.choice: A non-determinism violation is precisely 'the replayed command does not line up with the recorded history at the cursor's current position.' The cursor knows which recorded event is next; the command states what it expects; if family or correlation key differ, that is a violation — detected by exact comparison, not by guessing or tolerance. It is raised as a typed NonDeterminismError carrying workflow id, sequence position, expected command shape, and found event shape, then recorded once as a WorkflowFailed and the workflow fails deterministically. It is never swallowed, never guessed past, never allowed to produce a Resolution. Dedicated tests drive a deliberately divergent history.

decisions.title: workflow.now is recorded-timestamp only; workflow.random is seeded only
decisions.choice: The DeterminismContext exposes the recorded timestamp of the event currently being applied as the sole source for workflow.now, and a deterministic RNG seeded from WorkflowId + RunId as the sole source for workflow.random. No code path producing a workflow-visible value calls the wall clock or any entropy source. Two executions of the same run yield identical now and random sequences. The engine wall time used by recovery to evaluate expired_timers is explicitly not a workflow-visible value. This is the engine-side behaviour the AF Gleam bindings resolve to.

decisions.title: The history is the activity-result cache; no separate cache store
decisions.choice: On replay a matched ActivityCompleted returns its recorded result Payload directly (the activity is never dispatched); a matched exhausted-retry ActivityFailed returns the recorded terminal ActivityError; recorded non-exhausting failed attempts are walked past as part of the recorded retry sequence. There is no separate cache structure — the HistoryCursor over recorded events is the cache. Rejected a side cache keyed by activity id: it would duplicate the source of truth and risk drift from the log.

decisions.title: Replay drives to a resume point; the LiveExecutor seam takes over there
decisions.choice: replay builds a Resolver from read_history and drives the freshly-created execution forward, satisfying every command from the cursor, until the cursor is exhausted (ResumeLive) — that single command is the handoff. Before the handoff the LiveExecutor is never called (CO11); after it, live actions execute and the Recorder appends new events. Recovery is replay-and-resume applied to every workflow from list_active. Rejected re-executing recorded side effects and discarding the results: it would double every side effect and defeat durability.

decisions.title: AD defines event-family replay; AT defines live behaviour
decisions.choice: For timers, signals, child workflows, and concurrency: AT (when live) performs the action and asks the Recorder to append the corresponding events; AD (during replay) consults the cursor and returns the recorded outcome without invoking AT — a fired timer is skipped instantly, a received signal delivered immediately, a completed child returns its recorded result. AD owns the Resolution shapes and matching rules for these families and tests them against recorded histories directly, never needing AT. The resume point is the single handoff where AT takes over.

constraints.description: unsafe_code = deny across the whole module set.
No #[allow]/#[expect]/#[ignore] bypasses; tests use InMemoryStore in-process, never env-gated skips.
mod.rs holds only pub mod declarations and re-exports; logic lives in named files.
500-line file limit (excluding tests/comments/whitespace); split the Resolver and replay driver before they exceed it.
aion::durability depends only on aion-core and aion-store (plus an RNG crate and chrono); no AT, AF, networking, or direct beamr — runtime concerns are reached only through the LiveExecutor trait.

Single-writer discipline is structural: only a Recorder appends to a workflow's history, exactly one Recorder per active workflow; no other path calls EventStore::append for a workflow with a live Recorder.

Every append states an expected_seq from the Recorder's tracked head; the head is never guessed or read from outside the Recorder, and a SequenceConflict is surfaced as a hard error (double-writer bug), not retried by re-reading.

workflow.now derives only from the recorded event timestamp in the DeterminismContext; no code path here calls the wall clock to produce a workflow-visible value. The recovery wall time for expired_timers is explicitly not workflow-visible.
workflow.random derives only from the seed (WorkflowId + RunId); no entropy source is consulted; same run -> identical random sequence.
A non-determinism violation is always raised as a typed NonDeterminismError at the point of mismatch; never swallowed, never guessed past, never allowed to produce a Resolution.
Replay never invokes a live side effect: while the cursor can satisfy a command the LiveExecutor is not called; it is called only after ResumeLive.
Determinism-violation detection has dedicated test coverage: a recorded history that deliberately diverges from the replayed command stream asserts the typed error.

goals: A Recorder that tracks a workflow's sequence head and is the single writer of its history, appending each event family (lifecycle, activity, timer, signal, child) with the correct expected_seq.
A HistoryCursor that walks recorded events in sequence order and resolves the next recorded event of a given family and correlation key, reporting exhaustion.
Correlation keys and matching rules: activities/children by scheduling ordinal, timers by TimerId, signals by name-in-order.
Command and Resolution types forming the AD seam, plus a Resolver that returns recorded/resume-live/violation against the cursor.
A NonDeterminismError + DurabilityError taxonomy (thiserror), violations carrying workflow id, sequence position, expected and found shapes.
A DeterminismContext exposing recorded-now and a WorkflowId+RunId-seeded RNG; identical outputs across replays of the same run.
A LiveExecutor trait (the AD/AE handoff) invoked only on resume-live, plus the glue that records new events after handoff.
A replay driver that builds a Resolver from read_history and drives an execution to the resume point, satisfying activity/timer/signal/child commands from history and caching activity results.
A recovery routine that lists active workflows on startup and replays-and-resumes each.
Dedicated determinism-violation tests over InMemoryStore plus replay/recorder behavioural tests.
non_goals: No live execution of timers, signals, queries, or child workflows (AT) — AD defines only how their recorded forms replay.
No workflow lifecycle, process spawning, supervision, or module loading (AE) — AD is invoked through the Recorder/Resolver/replay/recover surface and the LiveExecutor seam.
No Gleam SDK surface (AF) — AD defines only the engine-side behaviour workflow.now/workflow.random bind to.
No storage backend — AD uses the EventStore trait and tests against InMemoryStore (AC); libSQL is AS, PostgreSQL is AX.
No event compaction or snapshotting — replay always consumes full history; truncation is a later measured concern.
No retry-policy definition — the policy lives with the activity contract and its application is AE/AT; AD only replays the recorded outcome of retries that already happened.

structure.crates/aion/src/durability/recorder.rs: [AD-002] Recorder: single-writer append path over EventStore
structure.crates/aion/src/durability/executor.rs: [AD-007] LiveExecutor trait (AE implements) + resume-live handoff glue
structure.crates/aion/src/durability/replay.rs: [AD-008] replay driver to the resume point + activity-result caching
